title: The fractal dimension of Brownian dynamics in liquids
authors: Michael C. Thumann, Jason Boynewicz, Giuseppe Procopio, Massimiliano Giona, Mark G. Raizen
abstract: The classical Einstein-Langevin theory of Brownian motion assumes a memoryless thermal bath, establishing a universal fractal dimension of $d_v = 3/2$ for the velocity fluctuations of a particle. In this Letter, we demonstrate experimentally and theoretically that fluid-inertial memory effects fundamentally redefine the fractal scaling of these fluctuations. In analyzing highly resolved measurements of Brownian microspheres in liquids, we show that the non-Markovian hydrodynamic thermal noise establishes a distinct velocity fractal dimension of $d_v = 7/4$. Coupled with theoretical analysis of non-equilibrium short-time dynamics and the initial scaling of the velocity autocorrelation function, this result establishes the non-equilibrium universality class of Brownian motion in fluid media possessing a finite non-vanishing density.
summary: This paper investigates how fluid inertia modifies the expected fractal scaling of Brownian motion, moving beyond the classical Einstein-Langevin prediction. Through a combination of high-resolution experiments and advanced theoretical modeling, the authors demonstrate that the true velocity fractal dimension is $d_v = 7/4$. This work establishes a non-equilibrium universality class for particle dynamics in viscous fluids.
main_problem: To revise the understanding of the fractal dimension of Brownian velocity fluctuations, moving beyond the classical Einstein-Langevin theory prediction.
main_result: The inclusion of fluid-inertial memory effects fundamentally changes the scaling, establishing a distinct velocity fractal dimension of $d_v = 7/4$ instead of the classical $3/2$.
method: The study combines experimental measurements of microspheres in liquids with theoretical analysis using generalized Langevin equations and scale-dependent fluctuation analysis.
model_or_system: The system is Brownian microspheres moving in a liquid, modeled by incorporating fluid-inertial memory effects via the Basset kernel, moving beyond the simple Markovian Langevin assumption.
key_observables: Velocity fractal dimension ($d_v$), scaling exponent $\phi$ from Mean Squared Displacement ($	ext{MSD} \sim 	au^\phi$), and the scale-dependent absolute slope ($S_v(\Delta t)$).
important_parameters: The analysis distinguishes between the classical (Einstein-Langevin) and hydrodynamic (Basset-Boussinesq) regimes, focusing on short-time, non-equilibrium dynamics.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on relating scaling exponents ($\phi, H_v, d_v$) and requires careful handling of short-time singularities and experimental noise/resolution limits.
figures_summary: Figures compare scaling functions ($L_x, S_x, L_v, S_v$) for different models, showing crossovers in scaling behavior and confirming the $d_v = 7/4$ result from experimental data.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the classical theory, introducing the non-Markovian hydrodynamic model, developing advanced measurement techniques (like $S_v(\Delta t)$), and finally deriving the non-equilibrium universality class using scaling analysis.
why_interesting: The work provides a detailed, quantitative study of non-equilibrium dynamics in a classical system, using advanced scaling analysis techniques that are highly relevant to understanding memory effects in open quantum systems or complex many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Normalized velocity u(τ) vs τ of a barium titanate glass microsphere in acetone.

FIG. 2. Hydrodynamic fractal scaling of Brownian velocity ﬂuctuations. The black circles are Sv(∆t) from experimental data of a barium titanate glass microsphere optically trapped in acetone after accounting for laser noise and ﬁnite experi- mental resolution as detailed in Appendix H. Statistical error bars [36] are smaller than the marker size, and parametric uncertainty is detailed in Appendix H. The solid blue line is Sv(∆t) as predicted by the hydrodynamic GLE. The grey dashed line is the asymptotic scaling ∆t−3/4. The inset shows the scaling of Lu(∆τ) vs τ using raw data without laser noise or ﬁnite-time corrections (•). Line (a) represents the scaling ∆τ −3/4, line (b) ∆τ −1/2.

FIG. 3. Initial scaling of 1 −C(n) uu (τ) vs ∆τ of the solutions of the inertial model eq. (14) at κs = 0, β = 1 for diﬀerent values of Γ. Symbols (•) refer to Γ = 1010, (▲) to Γ = 108, (■) to Γ = 106. Line (a) corresponds to 1 −C(n) uu (τ) ∼τ 1/2, line (b) to 1 −C(n) uu (τ) ∼τ.

FIG. 4. Length-resolution analysis Lu(∆τ) vs ∆τ of the ve- locity realizations solution of the inertial model eq. (14) at κs = 0.1, b = 0.1 for diﬀerent values of Γ. The arrow in- dicates increasing values of Γ = 104, 106, 108. Line (a) rep- resents the scaling Lu(∆τ) ∼∆τ −3/4, line (b) the scaling Lu(∆τ) ∼∆τ −1/2.
